title: Chapter 10: Laws of Survival
paragraphs: While Avotu raked the ashes of destroyed illusions in the deadly-silent throne hall, and Zhenya enthusiastically assembled pneumatic armor in the Guild, Yuri was methodically going crazy from idleness and growing panic. The city of Agdash was huge, architecturally beautiful in its decay, and... absolutely, unbearably chaotic.

The former office worker, whose brain was used to functioning within a strict schedule, Excel tables, reports, and flawless job descriptions, could not come to terms with how elven society functions in a state of permanent apocalypse. Logistics limped on both legs, the distribution of meager rations resembled a natural disaster, and workplaces looked like a gathering of unorganized suicides. For Yuri, this chaos was scarier than demons—it symbolized a loss of control. And a loss of control meant death.

Unable to bear sitting in the tavern, Yuri wandered through the gloomy, smog-shrouded industrial tiers. His feet led him to the edge of the Great Eastern Mine—a giant sinkhole on the outskirts of the city, where power ores that powered Agdash's worn-out protective barriers were mined. The sight before him was the last straw.

Hundreds of elves bent their backs, carrying overloaded baskets along flimsy, rotten wooden platforms. Ropes broke, rocks fell on heads, workers screamed in pain, and the overseers only helplessly waved their hands, hoping for the will of fate.
"This is a gross violation of basic safety techniques!" Yuri cried out, unable to help it, grabbing his head with both hands. "You don't have mining here, but an outright meat grinder!"
An elf, standing at the winch, looked at the twitchy, dusty man in wrinkled earthly trousers with tired, deep-sunken eyes.
"We need ore, stranger. The more, the better for our Agdash. The rest doesn't matter," the elf replied fatalistically, spitting gray coal dust under his feet.

"It does!" Yura was literally bursting. Animal fear of the unknown gave way to the icy, unshakable professional rage of a man who had written regulations all his life. "A dead worker doesn't mine ore! An injured worker slows down the rest and wastes resources! If you rebuild the logistics, you will double production, reducing losses of target personnel to zero!"

In the next few hours, Yuri, as if obsessed with the spirit of order, ran through the dirty tiers. He forced the overseers to strengthen the platforms with additional beams, strictly separated the flows of dirty and clean ore, and, most importantly, introduced an unshakeable schedule of shifts with breaks for water. The elves initially snapped at the 'human underdog', but when the first organized flow of heavy carts arrived at the surface three times faster than usual without a single hitch, the dissatisfied murmur fell silent.

By evening, an unusual, almost frightening, but effective order reigned at the mine. The shift supervisor, a tall gray-haired elf with a weathered face, approached Yuri, who was wiping acrid sweat from his forehead with a rag.

"Your methods are very strange, human. There isn't a drop of magic in them," the old man said thoughtfully, looking at the rhythmic work of the brigades. "But they have steel. From this day on, you're the foreman of the eastern slope. No one goes down into the mine without your knowledge and your signature."

Yuri exhaled with relief, looking at the winches working steadily like a clock. For the first time since falling into this cursed world, his heart stopped pounding from animal terror. Here, in the dust and noise of mechanisms, surrounded by strict regulations and rules that he himself had written, he found what his trauma-tormented consciousness was looking for—an absolute point of support in a collapsing universe.

Late at night, returning to the Mechanics' Guild, Yuri collided with Alexander. The natural leader looked tired to death, but his gaze was tenacious, scanning and clear. He listened to Yuri's excited story about the mine with a slight, sincere brotherly smile.

"You did well, Yura. You found your place. You heal yourself from fear with action," Alexander patted him on the shoulder. "We can't hide behind Avotu's back forever and wait for him to save us. To survive here, we must become part of the blood system of this world. Zhenya forges us weapons, you take control of city logistics, and I..." he raised a heavy look at the lights of the flickering palace. "I will try to become the diplomatic glue so that we aren't crushed here from above. This world doesn't forgive weakness. We will build our earthly rules of survival here."
